title: What is a map
instruction:
Think about a library card catalog.

You look up an ISBN like "978-0-14-044913-6" and find the book's title and author. You look up another ISBN and get that book's details. You do not search by shelf position — you search by ISBN.

A map works the same way. You look up a key (ISBN) and get its value (book info) back.

Run this. Then try looking up a different ISBN.
successMessage: Look up by ISBN, get the book title. That is the entire concept of a map.

title: Declare a map with make
instruction:
When you want an empty catalog to fill later, use `make`.

`make(map[string]int)` creates an empty map where keys are ISBNs and values are available copies.

The key type goes first, value type second — both inside the brackets.

Add two books with their available copy counts and print both.

codeChecks.message: Keep the make(map[string]int) declaration.
Add The Odyssey: copies["978-0-14-044913-6"] = 3
Add Mockingbird: copies["978-0-06-112008-4"] = 5
Print the map: fmt.Println(copies)
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: make(map[keyType]valueType) — empty catalog, ready to fill. Same make you used for slices.

title: Declare with values
instruction:
When you already have the catalog data, declare and fill in one shot.

```
copies := map[string]int{
    "978-0-14-044913-6": 3,
    "978-0-06-112008-4": 5,
}
```

Note the comma after the last value — Go requires it when each entry is on its own line.

Add two more ISBNs with copy counts to the map literal and print it.
hint: Add two more entries inside the curly braces, each on its own line with a comma at the end.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your two entries.

successMessage: Map literal syntax. Key: value, — the comma at the end of each line is required in Go when closing brace is on its own line.

title: Add and update a key
instruction:
Adding a new book and updating an existing book's copy count use the exact same syntax.

`copies["978-0-452-28423-4"] = 4` — if the ISBN does not exist, it is added. If it already exists, the value is replaced.

Go does not distinguish between insert and update. It is always just assignment.

Add a new book, then update an existing book's copy count, then print the map.

codeChecks.message: Add new book: copies["978-1-566-19005-5"] = 6
Update Odyssey: copies["978-0-14-044913-6"] = 7
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: Same line adds and updates. If the ISBN exists, the count changes. If not, the entry is created. One syntax, two behaviours.

title: Read a value
instruction:
Reading from a map returns the value — or the zero value if the key does not exist.

For `map[string]int`, a missing key returns `0`. For `map[string]string`, it returns `""`.

No error. No crash. Just silently zero.

Run this. Notice what prints for an ISBN that does not exist in the catalog.
successMessage: That unknown ISBN is not in the catalog — Go returned 0 instead of crashing. That is convenient but also dangerous: you cannot tell if the ISBN was missing or if the real count happened to be 0. That is what the next step fixes.

title: Check if a key exists
instruction:
The two-value assignment solves the ambiguity from the last step.

`count, ok := copies["978-0-99999999-9"]`

- `count` is the value (zero if key missing)
- `ok` is `true` if the ISBN exists, `false` if not

This is called the ok idiom. You will see it constantly in Go.

Add a check for an ISBN that exists and one that does not.
hint: count, ok := copies["978-0-14-044913-6"]  then fmt.Println(count, ok). Repeat for a missing ISBN.

codeChecks.message: Use the two-value form: count, ok := copies["978-0-14-044913-6"]
Also check for a missing ISBN: copies["978-0-99999999-9"]
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: count, ok — the ok idiom. It is Go's standard pattern for safe lookups. You will use this for maps, type assertions, channel reads, and more.

title: Delete a key
instruction:
`delete(map, key)` removes a key-value pair entirely.

If the ISBN does not exist, nothing happens — no error.

Delete two books from the catalog and print the result.

codeChecks.message: Delete Mockingbird: delete(copies, "978-0-06-112008-4")
Delete 1984: delete(copies, "978-0-452-28423-4")
Replace the TODO comment with your delete calls.

successMessage: delete() takes the map and the key. Two books removed. The remaining two are unaffected.

title: len on a map
instruction:
`len(map)` returns how many key-value pairs are in the map.

Add two books, delete one, and print the length after each operation.

codeChecks.message: Add Hobbit: catalog["978-0-618-00221-3"] = 8
Delete Mockingbird: delete(catalog, "978-0-06-112008-4")
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: len works on maps just like slices. It tells you how many books are currently in the catalog.

title: Loop over a map
instruction:
`for isbn, count := range myMap` iterates over every key-value pair.

Important: maps are unordered. Every time you loop, the books may come out in a different order. Go does this intentionally — do not rely on the order.

Loop through the catalog and print each entry as `"ISBN: count"`.
hint: for isbn, count := range catalog { fmt.Printf("%s: %d\n", isbn, count) }

codeChecks.message: Use range to loop: for isbn, count := range catalog
Use fmt.Printf to format the output.
Replace the TODO comment with your loop.

successMessage: Looping works. But notice the output order may vary each run — maps have no guaranteed order. If you need sorted output, sort the keys first.

title: Map with slice values
instruction:
A map's value can be any type — including a slice.

`map[string][]string` maps a member ID to their list of checked-out books.

This is perfect for tracking who has borrowed which books.

Add a third member with two checked-out books, then loop and print all of them.

codeChecks.message: Add member: loans["M003"] = []string{"...", "..."}
Replace the TODO comment with your assignment.

successMessage: map[string][]string — a map where each value is a slice. One member ID, many borrowed books. You will see this pattern a lot.

title: Nested maps
instruction:
A map can also hold other maps.

`map[string]map[string]int` — library branch name maps to ISBN maps to available copies.

Useful for hierarchical data: branch → ISBN → count.

Add a third branch with two ISBNs and their available counts, then print the count of one specific book at that branch.

codeChecks.message: Add South branch: branchCopies["South"] = map[string]int{"ISBN": number, ...}
Access The Odyssey at South: branchCopies["South"]["978-0-14-044913-6"]
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: Nested maps. Access with double brackets: map[branch][isbn]. Useful for any hierarchical data like branch inventories.

title: Build a genre counter
instruction:
Classic map use case: count how many books belong to each genre.

Loop through the genres slice. For each genre, add 1 to its count in the map. If the genre is not in the map yet, Go returns 0 — so `genreCounts[genre] += 1` works perfectly on the first hit too.

Add the counting loop, then print the map.
hint: for _, genre := range genres { genreCounts[genre]++ }  then fmt.Println(genreCounts)

codeChecks.message: Increment the count for each genre: genreCounts[genre]++
Loop through the genres slice: for _, genre := range genres
Print genreCounts: fmt.Println(genreCounts)
Replace the TODO lines with your code.

successMessage: Genre counter done. This exact pattern powers library analytics, collection management dashboards, and circulation reports. Maps make it trivial.
